questions.text: Human cells maintain concentration gradients across their plasma membranes, such that there is a high sodium concentration outside the cell and a high potassium concentration inside the cell. Suppose that within the cell membrane are sodium leak channels or carrier proteins. These channels would allow sodium to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Move out of the cell by simple diffusion
options.explanation: Option A is wrong because neither sodium moves out of the cell nor is it transported via simple diffusion.

options.text: Move into the cell by simple diffusion
options.explanation: Option B is wrong because sodium does not transport via simple diffusion.

options.text: Move out of the cell by facilitated diffusion
options.explanation: Option C is wrong because sodium moves inside the cell, not out of the cell.

options.text: Move into the cell by facilitated diffusion
options.explanation: As the question mentions, there is a high concentration of sodium ions outside the cell, which means that through sodium leaky channels, the sodium ions should move inside the cell, not outside. The type of movement that occurs through channels or carrier proteins down the concentration gradient is called facilitated diffusion. That is why the answer is D.

questions.explanation: As the question mentions, there is a high concentration of sodium ions outside the cell, which means that through sodium leaky channels, the sodium ions should move inside the cell, not outside. The type of movement that occurs through channels or carrier proteins down the concentration gradient is called facilitated diffusion. That is why the answer is D.

questions.text: The least distance vision for a person with hypermetropia is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option suggests that the least distance vision for a person with hypermetropia is 25 cm. However, this is incorrect. Hypermetropic individuals typically have difficulty focusing on objects closer than this distance due to the refractive error. Their near point is further away.

This option suggests that the least distance vision for a person with hypermetropia is less than 25 cm. This is also incorrect. Hypermetropia causes difficulty in focusing on close objects, so the near point is beyond 25 cm.

C is the correct option because the least distance of distinct vision is 35cm. This is more than the least distance of distinct vision of an ordinary person. Hence, the person is suffering from Hypermetropia.

This option suggests that the least distance vision for a person with hypermetropia is infinity. While it is true that hypermetropes have better distance vision compared to near vision, it is not accurate to say that their least distance vision is infinite. The near point is a finite distance, just farther away than for individuals with normal vision.

questions.explanation: The closest distance we can see with our eyes is 25 cm. This is called the least distance of distinct vision, which is the closest distance the lens of our eye can focus light on the retina.Now, if the closest distance a person can see is 35 cm, then the least distance of distinct vision of the person has increased. This is called hypermetropia or farsightedness, where the person's least distance for distinct vision is increased, and they will have difficulty seeing things in the near, but they will not have any problem seeing things in the far distance.This condition happens because the image is formed behind the retina, not on the retina. Here, the eye lens focuses the incoming light from an object at a point behind the retina.A converging lens is useful to see his surroundings better. If we place a converging lens or convex lens between the eye and the object with the required converging power, then the light rays from the object will focus at the retina. That’s why a converging lens is used to see properly if someone has hypermetropia.

questions.text: The nerve center for sight is located in the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A is the correct option because the thalamus receives sensory information (i.e., electrical impulses) from the retina through the optic nerve. The retina is located at the back of the eyeball, and it contains nerve cells. When light strikes these nerve cells, the nerve cells will send electrical impulses through the optic nerve to the thalamus. Once the thalamus receives these electrical impulses, the thalamus will send these impulses to the cortex.
Option B is wrong because it is simply short-sighted, not able to see clearly things that are far away.
Option C is wrong because B is wrong.
Option D is wrong because option A is correct.

questions.explanation: In the visual system, the thalamus receives input from the retina, which is relayed to the brain via the optic nerve. B simply means short-sighted, hence incorrect.

questions.text: On land, frogs are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A and B are terms related to the CNS.
Option A and B are terms related to the CNS.
Option C is incorrect because frogs are near-sighted.

D is correct because on land, frogs are myopic. This is correct as the eyes of frogs are myopic on land, which means that their eyes are nearsighted, and the large, round lens of the frog gives the animal a large field of view. They are naturally nearsighted, which means myopic to -6 diopters, which gives the focus of approximately 6 inches.

questions.explanation: Options A and B are terms related to the Central Nervous System. Option C is incorrect because frogs are naturally near-sighted. D is correct because on land, frogs are myopic. This is correct as the eyes of frogs are myopic on land, which means that their eyes are nearsighted, and the large, round lens of the frog gives the animal a large field of view. They are naturally nearsighted, which means myopic to -6 diopters, which gives the focus of approximately 6 inches.

questions.text: The sense organs of taste in tongue are known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A is wrong because Olfactory receptors (ORs), also known as odorant receptors, are chemoreceptors expressed in the cell membranes of olfactory receptor neurons and are responsible for the detection of odorants (for example, compounds that have an odor), which give rise to the sense of smell.

B is the correct option because Gustatory receptors are the taste receptors that are present on the surface of the tongue in taste buds. Each taste bud consists of many taste receptor cells. The opening of each taste bud, taste pore, allows fluid in the mouth to come in contact with receptor cells.

Option C is wrong because a cutaneous receptor is the type of sensory receptor found in the skin ( the dermis or epidermis). They are a part of the somatosensory system. Cutaneous receptors include mechanoreceptors (pressure or distortion), nociceptors (pain), and thermoreceptors (temperature).
Option D is wrong because options A and C are wrong.

questions.explanation: The gustatory system, or sense of taste, is the sensory system that is partially responsible for the perception of taste (flavor). olfactory system, or sense of smell, is the sensory system used for smelling. Cutaneous receptors are a part of the somatosensory system, including mechanoreceptors and nociceptors.

questions.text: Which of the following types and functions of the neuron is correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Sensory neurons carry impulses from sensory receptors to the CNS; interneurons relay signals within the CNS; motor neurons transmit impulses from the CNS to muscles or glands
options.explanation: This option correctly describes the roles of sensory, interneurons, and motor neurons in the nervous system.

options.text: Motor neurons carry sensory impulses to the CNS; interneurons transmit impulses to muscles; sensory neurons relay signals within the CNS
options.explanation: This option incorrectly assigns the functions of motor, interneurons, and sensory neurons, mixing up their roles.

options.text: Interneurons carry impulses from effectors to the CNS; sensory neurons transmit impulses from the CNS to interneurons; motor neurons pass signals within the CNS
options.explanation: This option incorrectly describes the pathways of interneurons, sensory neurons, and motor neurons, misrepresenting their functions.

options.text: Sensory neurons transmit impulses from the CNS to effectors; motor neurons carry sensory information to the CNS; interneurons connect sensory to motor neurons outside the CNS
options.explanation: This option reverses the functions of sensory and motor neurons and misplaces the role of interneurons.

questions.explanation: Sensory neurons function to carry the sensory information to the CNS. Interneurons transfer impulses from sensory to motor neurons, remaining within the CNS. Motor neurons are neurons that transmit impulses fromthe CNS to the effectors.

questions.text: Which part of the brain regulates body temperature?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The cerebellum is primarily responsible for coordinating voluntary movements and balance. It does not regulate body temperature.
The cerebrum is the largest part of the brain, involved in higher brain functions like thought and action. It is not directly responsible for regulating body temperature.
The hypothalamus plays a crucial role in maintaining homeostasis, including regulating body temperature, hunger, thirst, and other autonomic functions.
The medulla controls autonomic functions such as heart rate and breathing, but it does not regulate body temperature.

questions.explanation: Much like a thermostat regulates the temperature inside your home, the hypothalamus regulates your body temperature, responding to internal and external stimuli and making adjustments to keep the body within one or two degrees of 98.6 degrees.

questions.text: Negative feedback mechanism is the characteristic of which class?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While fish do not have regulatory mechanisms to maintain homeostasis, the negative feedback mechanism is not exclusive to Class fish.
Amphibians also have regulatory mechanisms, but again, the negative feedback mechanism is not specific to Class Amphibia.
Reptilia, like fish and amphibians, have regulatory mechanisms to maintain homeostasis, but he negative feedback mechanism is not a defining characteristic of Class Reptilia.
The negative feedback mechanism is a regulatory process commonly found in mammals.

questions.explanation: Negative feedback is a type of regulation in biological systems in which the end product of a process, in turn, reduces the stimulus of that same process. Feedback, in general, is a regulatory mechanism present in many biological reactions. By allowing certain pathways to be turned off and on, the body can control various aspects of its internal environment. Examples of negative feedback include the regulation of blood glucose levels and the temperature regulation of the body.

questions.text: Which of the following occurs in the body in response to the secretion of Glucagon
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Conversion of glucose to glycogen in liver cells
options.explanation: Conversion of glucose to glycogen decreases blood glucose levels. It is a function of insulin.
A decrease in blood glucose level is achieved with insulin.

options.text: Increased uptake of glucose by muscle cells
options.explanation: Muscle cells cause glucose to be used while doing work. It is not a function of glucagon because glucagon causes an increase in the blood glucose level.

options.text: Production of cyclic AMP in target cells
options.explanation: 'Glucagon' has an opposite role to 'insulin', i.e., it acts to release blood glucose in the plasma when blood has a low level of glucose in it. When glucagon is released, it stimulates the liver to break down glycogen into glucose. The chief role of cyclic AMP in several tissues is to promote the mobilization of glucose and fatty acid reserves. In the liver, glucagon and catecholamines cause an increase in the intracellular level of cyclic AMP by stimulating adenylyl cyclase.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct. 'Glucagon' has the opposite role to 'insulin', i.e., it mobilizes blood glucose in the plasma when you’re hypoglycemic. When glucagon is released, it can perform the following tasks: Stimulating the liver to break down glycogen to be released into the blood as glucose. Activating gluconeogenesis is the conversion of amino acids into glucose. Break down stored fat (triglycerides) into fatty acids for use as fuel by cells. All of the other options, except D, explain the role of insulin and not glucagon. By simply using an elimination method, we can deduce that D is the right answer. Just a piece of additional information: The chief role of cyclic AMP in several tissues seems to be to facilitate or promote the mobilization of glucose and fatty acid reserves. In the liver, glucagon and catecholamines cause an increase in the intracellular level of cyclic AMP by stimulating adenylyl cyclase.

questions.text: The correct option about spinal nerves is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Spinal nerves have a total of 62 nerves, i.e., 31 pairs of nerves.
All the spinal nerves are mixed nerves.

options.text: The dorsal root contains sensory neurons
options.explanation: This option is correct. It is true that the dorsal root contains sensory neurons.

options.text: The ventral root contains motor neurons
options.explanation: This option is also correct. It is true that the ventral root contains motor neurons.
Since both options C and D are correct, Option E is the most suitable option.

questions.explanation: As the dorsal root emerges from the intervertebral neural foramina, it forms the dorsal root ganglion (DRG). The DRG contains the cell body of the sensory neurons. These neurons relay sensory neural messages from the periphery to the central nervous system (brain and spinal cord). The ventral (anterior) or motor root consists of axons from the lower motor neurons in the ventral horn of the spinal cord. The ventral roots typically have fewer fibers than the dorsal roots, but they are of a larger caliber because they contain thick axons originating from the large lower motor neurons.

questions.text: _ is sometimes given by injection as an emergency treatment in cardiac arrest.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acetylcholine is the neurotransmitter at neuromuscular junctions, at synapses in the ganglia of the visceral motor system. It is a major neurotransmitter outside the CNS, i.e., in the PNS.

Dopamine is a chemical released in the brain that makes you feel good. Having the right amount of dopamine is important both for your body and your brain. Dopamine helps nerve cells to send messages to each other.

Serotonin is a chemical that carries messages between nerve cells in the brain and throughout your body. Serotonin plays a key role in such body functions as mood, sleep, digestion, nausea, wound healing, bone health, and blood clotting.
Epinephrine is the primary drug administered during cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) to reverse cardiac arrest. Epinephrine increases arterial blood pressure and coronary perfusion during CPR.

questions.explanation: When CPR does not generate coronary perfusion of more than 15-20 mmHg, return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) rarely or never occurs. After more than a few minutes of cardiac arrest, arterial tone collapses, and epinephrine or another vasoconstrictor is essential for restoration of cardiac activity.

questions.text: Which one of the following is a neurotransmitter?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Dopamine is a neurotransmitter made in your brain. It plays a role as a “reward center” and in many body functions, including memory, movement, motivation, mood, attention, etc.

Hydrochloric acid plays an important role in your digestion and immunity. It helps break down protein and absorb essential nutrients, and it helps control viruses and bacteria that might otherwise infect your stomach.
Sodium ion (Na+) is the principal extracellular cation and solute and is essential for the generation of action potentials in nervous and cardiac tissue.
Calcium ions (Ca+) trigger contraction by reacting with regulatory proteins that, in the absence of calcium, prevent the interaction of actin and myosin.

questions.explanation: 'Dopamine' is a neurotransmitter that helps send signals in the brain. Neurotransmitters are chemicals made by nerve cells called neurons. They're used to communicate messages across different parts of the brain and between the brain and the rest of the body. 'Dopamine' is involved mainly in controlling movement.

questions.text: Which brain portion is responsible for controlling body coordination?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The medulla oblongata helps control vital processes like your heartbeat, breathing, and blood pressure.
The main job of the amygdala is to regulate emotions, such as fear and aggression. The amygdala is also involved in tying emotional meaning to our memories, reward processing, and decision-making.
The cerebellum is part of your brain that helps coordinate and regulate a wide range of functions and processes in both your brain and body. The cerebellum controls balance and movement, and posture.
The pons is a part of your brainstem, a structure that links your brain to your spinal cord. It handles unconscious processes such as your sleep-wake cycle and breathing.

questions.explanation: The cerebellum, in the back of the brain, controls balance, coordination, and fine muscle control (e.g., walking). It also functions to maintain posture and equilibrium.

questions.text: Reabsorption of calcium is triggered by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Aldosterone triggers the reabsorption of sodium. Aldosterone is a hormone produced by the adrenal glands, which are small glands located on top of each kidney. It is classified as a mineralocorticoid hormone and plays a crucial role in regulating the balance of electrolytes and fluid in the body.

Parathormone triggers the reabsorption of calcium. Parathormone, also known as parathyroid hormone (PTH), is a hormone produced by the parathyroid glands, which are small glands located near the thyroid gland in the neck. Parathormone plays a key role in regulating calcium and phosphate levels in the blood and bone.

ADH triggers reabsorption of Antidiuretic hormone (ADH), also known as vasopressin, is a hormone produced by the hypothalamus and released by the posterior pituitary gland. ADH plays a crucial role in regulating the body's water balance and urine production.

Vasopressin is another name for ADH. Vasopressin, also known as antidiuretic hormone (ADH), is a hormone produced by the hypothalamus and released by the posterior pituitary gland. It plays a crucial role in regulating blood pressure and fluid balance in the body.

questions.explanation: Aldosterone triggers the reabsorption of sodium. Parathormone triggers the reabsorption of calcium. ADH triggers reabsorption of water. Vasopressin is another name for ADH.

questions.text: The _ is the main nutritional part and it's concerned with the biosynthesis of materials necessary for neurons.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nerves are like cables that carry electrical impulses between your brain and the rest of your body. Nerves are bundles of specialized cells called neurons that transmit electrical signals throughout the body. Neurons are the fundamental building blocks of the nervous system, which includes the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves.

Axon is a nerve fibre that carries signals from the cell body to the presynaptic membrane. The axon is a long, slender projection of a neuron that carries electrical signals, known as action potentials, away from the cell body towards other neurons, muscle cells, or glandular cells. It is a crucial component of the nervous system and plays a vital role in transmitting information and coordinating various physiological processes.

Soma is the cell body of the nerve cell, which contains most of the cell organelles. The soma, also known as the cell body or perikaryon, is the main component of a neuron that contains the nucleus and other essential organelles. It serves as the metabolic and genetic center of the neuron, playing a crucial role in maintaining its overall function and survival.

Dendrites are nerve fibres that carry signals from the postsynaptic membrane/receptor to the cell body. Dendrites are the branched extensions of a neuron that receive incoming signals from other neurons or sensory receptors. They are an essential part of the neuron's structure and function, serving as the primary site for synaptic input and information integration.

questions.explanation: Also known as the soma, the cell body is the neuron's core. The cell body carries genetic information, maintains the neuron's structure, and provides energy to drive activities. Like other cell bodies, a neuron's soma contains a nucleus and specialized organelles.

questions.text: Direction of reflex action is from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Receptor to sensory neuron to motor neuron and then through associative neurons to effect
options.explanation: Signal passes through associative neurons before reaching motor neurons.

options.text: Receptor to sensory neuron to associative neuron and then through motor neurons to effector
options.explanation: Stimulus received by the receptor and signal sent through the sensory neuron to associative neurons and then to motor neurons to the effector to perform the effect.

options.text: Effector to sensory neuron to associative neuron and then through motor neurons to Receptor
options.explanation: Stimulus received by the receptor and signal sent through the sensory neuron to associative neurons and then to motor neurons to the effector to perform the effect.

options.text: Effector to sensory neuron to motor neuron and then through associative neurons to Receptor
options.explanation: Stimulus received by the receptor and signal sent through the sensory neuron to associative neurons and then to motor neurons to the effector to perform the effect.

questions.explanation: A receptor in the skin detects a stimulus (the change in temperature). A sensory neuron sends electrical impulses to a relay neuron, which is located in the spinal cord of the CNS. Relay neurons connect sensory neurons to motor neurons. Motor neurons send electrical impulses to an effector. The effector produces a response (the muscle contracts to move the hand away).

questions.text: Resting membrane potential of neurons is _, while during active membrane potential the inner membrane surface becomes more _ than the outside.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The resting membrane potential is -70mV, and due to the inflow of Na+ causes the outer membrane becomes more positive.
The influx of Na+ causes the outer membrane to become more positive.
The resting membrane potential is -70mV.

Resting membrane potential is maintained at -70mV by multiple channels and due to the influx of Na+, causing the outer membrane to become more positive. The resting membrane potential of neurons is typically around -70 millivolts (mV). This means that the inside of the neuron is negatively charged relative to the outside. It is maintained by the unequal distribution of ions across the neuronal membrane and the selective permeability of the membrane to different ions. During active membrane potential, the inner membrane surface becomes more positive than the outside.

questions.explanation: During the resting state, the membrane potential arises because the membrane is predominantly permeable to K+. An action potential begins at the axon hillock as a result of depolarisation. During depolarisation, voltage-gated sodium ion channels open due to an electrical stimulus. During active membrane potential, the inner membrane surface becomes more positive than the outside.

questions.text: Auditory relay center is present in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The "left cerebral hemisphere" is primarily responsible for language processing and motor control of the right side of the body. However, the auditory relay centre, also known as the auditory pathway, is not specifically located in the "left cerebral hemisphere". It involves several structures in the brainstem and thalamus. Therefore, Option A is not correct.

The auditory relay centre is located in the midbrain. It receives auditory information from the ears and relays it to other brain parts for processing and interpretation. This helps in the coordination and control of hearing and sound localization. Therefore, Option B is the correct answer.

The hippocampus is a structure in the brain that is primarily associated with memory formation and spatial navigation. It is not directly involved in auditory processing or relay. Therefore, Option C is not correct.

The hindbrain, which includes structures such as the medulla, pons, and cerebellum, is primarily involved in regulating vital functions and coordinating movement. While the hindbrain plays a role in auditory processing, the specific auditory relay centre is not in the hindbrain. Therefore, Option D is not correct.

questions.explanation: The auditory relay centre is located in the "midbrain". It receives auditory information from the ears and relays it to other brain parts for processing and interpretation. This helps in the coordination and control of hearing and sound localization.

questions.text: During active membrane potential, _ conducts the impulse in the form of nerve impulse.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The axon is a long, slender projection of a neuron that conducts electrical impulses away from the cell body. It transmits nerve impulses, or action potentials, from the cell body to the target cells, such as other neurons, muscles, or glands. The axon is specialized for rapid and efficient signal transmission.

The soma, also known as the cell body or perikaryon, is the main part of the neuron that contains the nucleus and other cellular organelles. While the soma plays a vital role in maintaining the overall functioning and health of the neuron, it is not directly involved in conducting nerve impulses.

The neuron is the basic unit of the nervous system and consists of three main parts: the cell body (soma), dendrites, and axon. During active membrane potential, the neuron conducts an electrical impulse in the form of a nerve impulse or action potential. The impulse travels along the axon, a long, thin extension of the neuron, and is transmitted to other neurons, muscles, or glands.

Cytoplasm refers to the fluid-filled interior of a cell that surrounds the organelles. While the cytoplasm is involved in various cellular processes, it does not conduct nerve impulses directly. Nerve impulses are primarily conducted through the axon, a specific part of the neuron.

Ribosomes are cellular organelles responsible for protein synthesis. They play a crucial role in producing proteins essential for various cellular functions. However, ribosomes are not directly involved in conducting nerve impulses.

questions.explanation: The neuron is the basic unit of the nervous system and consists of three main parts: the cell body (soma), dendrites, and axon. During active membrane potential, the neuron conducts an electrical impulse in the form of a nerve impulse or action potential. The impulse travels along the axon, a long, thin extension of the neuron, and is transmitted to other neurons, muscles, or glands.

questions.text: Norepinephrine:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Norepinephrine, also known as noradrenaline, is a neurotransmitter and hormone released by sympathetic fibers of the autonomic nervous system. Its effects on the body include increasing heart rate, constricting blood vessels, and raising blood pressure.

Norepinephrine is not typically released by parasympathetic fibers. The parasympathetic nervous system is responsible for promoting relaxation and restful states, and it generally releases a different neurotransmitter called acetylcholine.

Norepinephrine does not decrease the heart rate. In fact, it has the opposite effect. Norepinephrine increases the heart rate as part of the sympathetic response, which prepares the body for increased activity and alertness.

Norepinephrine does not decrease blood pressure. It tends to increase blood pressure by constricting blood vessels and promoting vasoconstriction. This is part of the sympathetic response to enhance blood flow to essential organs during stress or physical activity.

options.text: Released by sympathetic fibers and decreases blood pressure
options.explanation: This option is partially correct. Norepinephrine is indeed released by sympathetic fibers, but it does not decrease blood pressure. As mentioned earlier, it tends to increase blood pressure by its actions on blood vessels and the heart.

questions.explanation: Norepinephrine, also known as noradrenaline, is a neurotransmitter and hormone released by sympathetic fibers of the autonomic nervous system. Its effects on the body include increasing heart rate, constricting blood vessels, and raising blood pressure.

questions.text: Hypothalamus initiates the release of hormones, by their releasing factors, while _ is directly produced by it.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone) is released by the anterior pituitary gland in response to a releasing factor called thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH) from the hypothalamus.
Oxytocin is released directly by the hypothalamus into the bloodstream. It is involved in childbirth, breastfeeding, and social bonding.

ACTH (adrenocorticotropic hormone) is released by the anterior pituitary gland in response to a releasing factor called corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) from the hypothalamus. ACTH stimulates the release of cortisol from the adrenal glands.

FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone) and LH (luteinizing hormone) are released by the anterior pituitary gland in response to releasing factors called follicle-stimulating hormone-releasing hormone (FSHRH) and luteinizing hormone-releasing hormone (LHRH) from the hypothalamus. FSH and LH are involved in the regulation of reproduction.

GH (growth hormone) is released by the anterior pituitary gland in response to a releasing factor called growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH) from the hypothalamus. GHRH stimulates the release of GH, which is involved in growth and development.

questions.explanation: Option A: TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone) is released by the anterior pituitary gland in response to a releasing factor called thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH) from the hypothalamus. Option B: Oxytocin is released directly by the hypothalamus into the bloodstream. It is involved in childbirth, breastfeeding, and social bonding. Option C: ACTH (adrenocorticotropic hormone) is released by the anterior pituitary gland in response to a releasing factor called corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) from the hypothalamus. ACTH stimulates the release of cortisol from the adrenal glands. Option D: FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone) and LH (luteinizing hormone) are released by the anterior pituitary gland in response to releasing factors called follicle-stimulating hormone-releasing hormone (FSHRH) and luteinizing hormone-releasing hormone (LHRH) from the hypothalamus. FSH and LH are involved in the regulation of reproduction. Option E: GH (growth hormone) is released by the anterior pituitary gland in response to a releasing factor called growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH) from the hypothalamus. GHRH stimulates the release of GH, which is involved in growth and development.

questions.text: Functions of the brainstem include all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The brainstem plays a crucial role in integrating and coordinating reflexes that help maintain an upright posture and balance in response to changes in body position.
The brainstem contains respiratory centers that regulate and control the automatic process of breathing, including the rhythm and depth of respiration.

The brainstem, along with other brain structures, contributes to the maintenance of balance, coordination, and posture. It receives sensory information from the inner ear and coordinates motor responses to help keep the body stable and oriented.

The initiation of voluntary movements primarily involves higher brain regions, such as the motor cortex. While the brainstem is involved in coordinating and refining movements, it does not directly initiate voluntary movements.

The brainstem includes structures responsible for controlling eye movements and coordinating the fixation of gaze. It ensures that the eyes can move together smoothly and accurately to focus on specific targets.

questions.explanation: Option A: Integration of righting reflexes: The brainstem plays a crucial role in integrating and coordinating reflexes that help maintain an upright posture and balance in response to changes in body position. Option B: Autonomic control for respiration: The brainstem contains respiratory centers that regulate and control the automatic process of breathing, including the rhythm and depth of respiration. Option C: Equilibrium and posture regulation: The brainstem, along with other brain structures, contributes to the maintenance of balance, coordination, and posture. It receives sensory information from the inner ear and coordinates motor responses to help keep the body stable and oriented. Option D: Initiation of voluntary movements: The initiation of voluntary movements primarily involves higher brain regions, such as the motor cortex. While the brainstem is involved in coordinating and refining movements, it does not directly initiate voluntary movements. Option E: Fixation of the eyes: The brainstem includes structures responsible for controlling eye movements and coordinating the fixation of gaze. It ensures that the eyes can move together smoothly and accurately to focus on specific targets.

questions.text: What part of the brain integrates sensory information such as pain and temperature?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The forebrain is the part of the brain that is responsible for higher-level functions, such as thinking, planning, and decision-making. It includes the thalamus, hypothalamus, amygdala, hippocampus, and cerebral cortex. The part of the brain that integrates sensory information, such as pain and temperature, is the hypothalamus.
The midbrain is the part of the brain that is responsible for connecting the forebrain and hindbrain. It includes the tectum, tegmentum, and substantia nigra.
The hindbrain is the part of the brain that is responsible for basic functions, such as breathing, heart rate, and balance. It includes the medulla oblongata, pons, and cerebellum.

The spinal cord is a long, thin bundle of nerves that runs from the brainstem to the lower back. It is responsible for transmitting sensory information from the body to the brain and for sending motor commands from the brain to the body.

questions.explanation: Option A: Forebrain is the part of the brain that is responsible for higher-level functions, such as thinking, planning, and decision-making. It includes the thalamus, hypothalamus, amygdala, hippocampus, and cerebral cortex. The part of the brain that integrates sensory information, such as pain and temperature, is the thalamus. The thalamus is a relay center for sensory information, meaning that it receives information from sensory receptors throughout the body and then sends it to the appropriate parts of the brain for processing. The thalamus is located in the forebrain, specifically in the diencephalon. Option B: The Midbrain is the part of the brain that is responsible for connecting the forebrain and hindbrain. It includes the tectum, tegmentum, and substantia nigra. Option C: The Hindbrain is the part of the brain that is responsible for basic functions, such as breathing, heart rate, and balance. It includes the medulla oblongata, pons, and cerebellum. Option D: The Spinal cord is a long, thin bundle of nerves that runs from the brainstem to the lower back. It is responsible for transmitting sensory information from the body to the brain and for sending motor commands from the brain to the body.

questions.text: Glands and muscles are activated by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sensory neurons receive information via their receptors, which are part of the peripheral nervous system, and convert this information into electrical impulses.
Associative neurons integrate information from sensory neurons and send signals to motor neurons.
Relay neurons transmit signals from one part of the CNS to another.

Motor neurons activate glands and muscles. Motor neurons are the final pathway in the nervous system that controls the contraction of muscles and the secretion of glands. They receive signals from associative neurons and send signals to the muscles and glands. The signals from motor neurons are called motor impulses. Motor impulses cause muscles to contract and glands to secrete.

questions.explanation: Option A: Sensory neurons transmit information from the environment to the central nervous system (CNS). Option B: Associative neurons integrate information from sensory neurons and send signals to motor neurons. Option C: Relay neurons transmit signals from one part of the CNS to another. Option D: Motor neurons activate glands and muscles. Motor neurons are the final pathway in the nervous system that controls the contraction of muscles and the secretion of glands. They receive signals from associative neurons and send signals to the muscles and glands. The signals from motor neurons are called motor impulses. Motor impulses cause muscles to contract and glands to secrete.

questions.text: The system involved in preparing our body in condition of stress and emergency:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The nervous system is responsible for detecting danger and sending signals to the body to prepare for a fight-or-flight response.

The endocrine system then releases hormones that help the body respond to stress. These hormones, such as adrenaline and cortisol, increase heart rate, blood pressure, and blood sugar levels. They also prepare the body for physical activity by increasing muscle strength and endurance.

The nervous system is responsible for detecting danger and sending signals to the body to prepare for a fight-or-flight response. The endocrine system then releases hormones that help the body respond to stress. These hormones, such as adrenaline and cortisol, increase heart rate, blood pressure, and blood sugar levels. They also prepare the body for physical activity by increasing muscle strength and endurance.

The endocrine system then releases hormones that help the body respond to stress. These hormones, such as adrenaline and cortisol, increase heart rate, blood pressure, and blood sugar levels. They also prepare the body for physical activity by increasing muscle strength and endurance. The exocrine system is not involved in the body's response to stress. The exocrine system is responsible for releasing substances, such as sweat and tears, through ducts. These substances do not have a direct role in the fight-or-flight response.

questions.explanation: The answer is both nervous and endocrine system. The nervous system is responsible for detecting danger and sending signals to the body to prepare for a fight-or-flight response. The endocrine system then releases hormones that help the body to respond to the stress. These hormones, such as adrenaline and cortisol, increase heart rate, blood pressure, and blood sugar levels. They also prepare the body for physical activity by increasing muscle strength and endurance.

questions.text: Which of the following helps in maintaining balance?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hypothalamus maintains homeostasis and has control centers for regulating hunger, sleep, thirst, body temperature, water balance, blood pressure, menstrual cycle, and sleep and wake cycle.
The medulla controls the automatic functions of the body, such as heartbeat, respiration, and swallowing.

The cerebellum controls equilibrium, i.e, body position and coordination of the actions of individual muscles to produce complex activities such as walking, running, riding bicycles, and doing delicate work with hands. The cerebellum is also involved in learning, memory storage for behavior.
Pons acts as a bridge between the cerebellum, medulla, and cerebrum. It controls the rate and pattern of heartbeat and breathing.

questions.explanation: The cerebellum is a part of the hindbrain that helps in maintaining the posture and balance of the body. The hypothalamus has several endocrine functions involving thermoregulation. Pons regulate the sleep-wake cycle. Medulla Oblongata controls involuntary movements such as breathing, heart rate, etc.

questions.text: Sensation of pleasure, punishment, or sexual arousal are stimulated by the parts of the brain:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The hippocampus consists of two “horns” that curve back from the amygdala. It appears to be very important in converting things that are “in your mind” at the moment(in short-term memory) into things that you will remember for the long run(long-term memory).
Hypothalamus maintains homeostasis and controls centers for regulating hunger, sleep, thirst, body temperature, water balance, blood pressure, menstrual cycle, and the sleep and wake cycle.
Amygdala are two almond-shaped masses of neurons on either side of the thalamus. They control feelings and emotions of love, hate, anger, fear, rage, and sexual arousal.
The thalamus is below the cerebrum. It receives all sensory impulses except the sense of smell and channels them to the limbic system and to the appropriate region of the cortex for interpretation.

questions.explanation: The amygdala controls the sensation of pleasure, punishment, or sexual arousal. The hippocampus has a major role in learning and memory. The hypothalamus is considered to be a link structure between the nervous and the endocrine system; its main function is to maintain the homeostasis of the body. The thalamus has several functions, such as relaying of sensory signals, including motor signals, to the cerebral cortex and the regulation of consciousness, sleep, and alertness.

questions.text: Goiter is one of the abnormalities due to a deficiency of a hormone:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Adrenaline is produced by the adrenal glands and is involved in the body's 'fight or flight' response, not in thyroid function.
Thyroxine is a hormone produced by the thyroid gland, and its deficiency can lead to goiter, a condition characterized by an enlarged thyroid gland.
Oxytocin is a hormone involved in childbirth and lactation, not in thyroid function or goiter development.
Parathormone is produced by the parathyroid glands and regulates calcium levels in the body. It is not related to goiter formation.

questions.explanation: The most common cause of goitres worldwide is a lack of iodine in the diet. In the United States, where the use of iodized salt is common, a goitre is more often due to the over- or under-production of thyroid hormones or to nodules in the gland itself.

questions.text: On receiving the news of the sudden demise of a close friend; which is the most probable hormone that is secreted at that time?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The prostaglandins are a group of lipids made at sites of tissue damage or infection that are involved in dealing with injury and illness. They control processes such as inflammation, blood flow, the formation of blood clots, and the induction of labour.
Growth inhibitors of various types have been identified in plants. The best characterized one is abscisic acid, which is chemically related to the cytokinins.
Estrogen is a category of sex hormones responsible for the development and regulation of the female reproductive system and secondary sexual characteristics.
Adrenaline is produced in the adrenal glands, which release adrenaline into the body during times of stress or danger. It prepares your body to face a stressful “fight or flight” situation.

Thyroxine is a hormone that the thyroid gland secretes into the bloodstream. Thyroxine plays a crucial role in heart and digestive function, metabolism, brain development, bone health, and muscle control. It affects almost all of the body's systems, which means proper thyroxine levels are vital for health.

questions.explanation: When a close person dies, this can cause the adrenal glands to pour adrenaline hormones into the bloodstream due to anxiety that subsides the emotional stress.

questions.text: Which of the following associations between the hormone and its classification is FALSE?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cortisol is released from the adrenal cortex and classified as a glucocorticoid.
Aldosterone is released from the adrenal cortex and is a principal mineralocorticoid.

Antidiuretic hormone (ADH), also known as vasopressin, is not a mineralocorticoid. ADH is a hormone produced by the hypothalamus and released by the posterior pituitary gland. Its primary function is to regulate water balance in the body by controlling the reabsorption of water in the kidneys.
Androgens are also released from the adrenal cortex and are sex hormones.

questions.explanation: ADH is not a mineralocorticoid. It is made by the hypothalamus and stored and released by the pituitary gland. Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) is a hormone that helps your kidneys manage the amount of water in your body.

questions.text: Which one is NOT true for coordination in animals?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Transmission by the nervous system is short-lived, whereas transmission by the hormonal system is long-lasting
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Nervous system signals are fast and short-lived, while hormonal signals are slower and longer-lasting.

options.text: The nervous system uses electrical impulses to send signals through neurons, whereas hormonal coordination uses chemical messengers transported in the blood
options.explanation: This statement is correct. The nervous system relies on electrical impulses, while hormones are chemical messengers circulated in the bloodstream.

options.text: Responses are often permanent in the hormonal system, but temporary and reversible in the nervous system
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Hormonal responses can have long-lasting effects, whereas nervous responses are typically quick and reversible.

options.text: In the nervous system, secretory chemicals are released in the blood, while in the hormonal system, secretory chemicals are released in extracellular fluids
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. In the nervous system, neurotransmitters are released in the synapse, not the blood. In contrast, hormones are released into the blood from glands.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D. In the nervous system, neurotransmitters are released at synapses between neurons, not into the bloodstream. Hormones, however, are released into the blood by endocrine glands. The other options correctly describe the characteristics of the nervous and hormonal systems in terms of signal duration, nature of transmission, and response permanence.

questions.text: What does the following picture show?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Motor neurons have their cell body attached directly to the dendrites without the presence of dendron processes, which are characteristic of sensory neurons.
This is correct. Sensory neurons have a cell body that connects to dendrites through dendron processes, as shown in the image.
This is incorrect. Interneurons typically have shorter processes that are comparable in size to the cell body, unlike what is shown in the image.
This is incorrect. A nerve consists of many neurons bundled together, whereas the image shows a single neuron.

questions.explanation: The diagram illustrates a sensory neuron. The defining feature is the cell body, which connects to the dendrites via dendron processes. This structure is specific to sensory neurons and is not found in motor neurons, where the cell body directly attaches to dendrites. Interneurons are characterized by processes that are similar in size to the cell body, which is not evident here. Lastly, the term 'nerve' refers to a collection of neurons, whereas the image shows only one neuron.

questions.text: All of the following are reflex actions EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Change in the size of the pupil in response to light
options.explanation: This is a reflex action known as the pupillary light reflex, where the pupil size adjusts involuntarily to regulate the amount of light entering the eye.

Swallowing is a complex process involving both voluntary and involuntary actions. Although the reflex action starts the process, the act of swallowing involves voluntary control, making it not a pure reflex action.

options.text: Sudden jerky withdrawal of hand when pricked
options.explanation: This action is a classic example of a reflex arc, where the body responds instantly and involuntarily to protect itself from harm by withdrawing the hand.

options.text: Knees jerk in response to a blow
options.explanation: This is known as the knee-jerk reflex, a sudden, involuntary response to a tap on the patellar tendon, demonstrating a reflex action.

questions.explanation: Reflex actions are involuntary and instantaneous responses to stimuli. The change in pupil size, jerky withdrawal of the hand, and knee jerk are all reflex actions because they occur automatically and without conscious thought. However, swallowing of the bolus, while it begins with a reflexive component, is primarily a voluntary action because it requires conscious control, especially after the initial trigger, making it not purely reflexive.

questions.text: Which is a correct description of the role of calcium ions in the neuromuscular system?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Exchanged with sodium ions through co-transport channels at axon surfaces during the reestablishment of a resting potential after an action potential
options.explanation: This process involves sodium and potassium ions, not calcium ions, and is related to restoring the resting potential rather than synaptic transmission.

options.text: Diffuses through gated ion channels in presynaptic membranes of excitatory neurons, prompting vesicles to move to the presynaptic membrane as an impulse arrives
options.explanation: The arrival of an impulse at the synaptic knob causes gated Ca2+ channels in the presynaptic membrane to open, allowing Ca2+ to enter the neuron. The influx of Ca2+ triggers vesicles containing neurotransmitters to move to and fuse with the presynaptic membrane, releasing their contents into the synaptic cleft via exocytosis.

options.text: Exchanged with chloride ions at the postsynaptic membrane, altering membrane potential in inhibitory neurons
options.explanation: This option confuses calcium ions with chloride ions, which are involved in inhibitory post-synaptic potentials, not in the presynaptic calcium influx.

options.text: Actively pumped out of axons at nodes of Ranvier in myelinated neurons, which is the main cause of the potential difference that is maintained during the resting potential
options.explanation: The maintenance of the resting potential involves the sodium-potassium pump, not calcium ions, and is unrelated to the role of calcium in neurotransmitter release.

questions.explanation: The role of calcium ions in the neuromuscular system is crucial during synaptic transmission. When an action potential reaches the synaptic knob, it triggers the opening of voltage-gated Ca2+ channels in the presynaptic membrane. Calcium ions then diffuse into the neuron, causing synaptic vesicles filled with neurotransmitters to move towards and fuse with the presynaptic membrane, leading to neurotransmitter release into the synaptic cleft by exocytosis. This process does not involve sodium or chloride ions, nor does it relate to the maintenance of resting potentials, which are handled by different ion channels and pumps.

questions.text: The synapse formed at the sites where the terminal branches of the axon of a motor neuron conduct to a target muscle cell is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This term refers to the receptor region on sensory neurons, not motor neurons, which is involved in receiving sensory stimuli rather than muscle innervation.
This is not a standard term in neuroscience; it does not refer to a recognized anatomical or functional structure.
This is the correct term for the neuromuscular junction, where motor neurons transmit signals to muscle cells, enabling muscle contraction.
This is the membrane on the receiving side of a synapse, not specific to neuromuscular junctions, and is common in neuron-to-neuron synapses.

questions.explanation: The motor end plate is the specialized area at the neuromuscular junction where the axon terminal of a motor neuron forms a synapse with a muscle fiber. It is responsible for transmitting neural signals to muscle cells, resulting in muscle contraction. The term sensory end plate is associated with sensory neurons, not motor neurons. Synapse end plate is not a recognized term in neuroscience. The postsynaptic membrane refers generically to the membrane receiving neurotransmitters at any synapse, not specifically at neuromuscular junctions.

questions.text: Acetylcholine is an example of _ .
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A neurohormone is any hormone produced and released by neuroendocrine cells into the blood, such as vasopressin or noradrenaline.
Acetylcholine is an excitatory neurotransmitter.
Hormones are chemical messengers that are secreted directly into the blood, which carries them to the organs and tissues of the body to exert their functions.

Pheromones are chemical substances that are created and emitted by organisms as odorants—often as oils or sweat—into the environment that may influence the behavior or physiology of other members of their species. This is particularly the case for mammals or insects.

questions.explanation: Acetylcholine is an excitatory neurotransmitter.

questions.text: Regulation of _ is NOT a function of hypothalamus in humans.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is the function of the hypothalamus.
It is the function of the hypothalamus.
It is the function of the hypothalamus.
It is not the function of the hypothalamus. It is the function of the pineal gland.

questions.explanation: The hypothalamus is the control centre for the regulation of blood water potential and urine osmolarity, mainly via the hormone ADH (antidiuretic hormone) and using the pituitary gland and collecting duct of the kidneys as the effector. The hypothalamus is the control centre for the regulation of body temperature. It is regulated by the thermoregulatory centre within the hypothalamus, which coordinates information to produce changes like vasoconstriction or vasodilation of arterioles supplying blood to the skin, controlling the production of sweat, and stimulating the secretion of hormones like adrenaline and thyroxine. Hypothalamus, however, does not regulate Circadian rhythms & emotions as circadian rhythm is regulated by the pineal gland, which produces melatonin, and emotions are regulated by the amygdala.

questions.text: Experience has no influence on this type of behaviour
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Kinesis is the movement of organisms towards or away from a strong stimulus, i.e., a flame or a projectile. This motion is innate and cannot be taught; many organisms know to move away from danger without being taught, hence they do not require experience.
This option is incorrect as imprinting is influenced by experience.
This option is incorrect as habituation is influenced by experience.
This option is incorrect as insight learning is influenced by experience.

questions.explanation: Kinesis is the movement of organisms towards or away from a strong stimulus, i.e., a flame or a projectile. This motion is innate and cannot be taught; many organisms know to move away from danger without being taught, hence they do not require experience.

questions.text: One of these processes does not happen as a result of a negative feedback mechanism in humans?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Secretion of insulin by the pancreas in response to increased blood glucose concentration
options.explanation: It is an example of negative feedback because when blood sugar rises, receptors in the body sense a change. In turn, the control center (pancreas) secretes insulin into the blood, effectively lowering blood sugar levels.

options.text: Secretion of oxytocin in response to dilation of the cervix during childbirth
options.explanation: It is positive feedback.

options.text: Secretion of glucagon by pancreas in response to decreased blood glucose concentration
options.explanation: It is a negative feedback because if the blood glucose level is too low, the pancreas releases the hormone glucagon. This travels to the liver in the blood and causes the breakdown of glycogen into glucose. The glucose enters the bloodstream, and glucose levels increase back to normal.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Options A and C are examples of negative feedback. In option A, when blood sugar rises, receptors in the body sense a change. In turn, the control center (pancreas) secretes insulin into the blood, effectively lowering blood sugar levels. In option C, if the blood glucose level is too low, the pancreas releases the hormone glucagon. This travels to the liver in the blood and causes the breakdown of glycogen into glucose. The glucose enters the bloodstream, and glucose levels increase back to normal. Option B is an example of positive feedback. Positive feedback amplifies or magnifies the change or output. The stimulus for oxytocin secretion is dilation of the uterine cervix, which signals the brain to release oxytocin from nerve endings in the posterior pituitary gland. The uterine contractions are further enhanced due to the oxytocin, which results in greater dilation of the cervix, which strengthens the signal for oxytocin release, and so on until the infant is expelled from the uterine cavity.

questions.text: When cocaine is used as a stimulant, it interferes with the CNS at the reuptake of which hormone?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Cocaine is a natural stimulant. Stimulants are drugs that stimulate the nervous system and increase alertness and excitement. Excessive dosage of cocaine can cause hallucinations. It interferes with the transportation of dopamine, a neurotransmitter. Dopamine is a chemical messenger and carries signals or nerve impulses from one brain cell to another. From one neuron to another, dopamine is passed through transporter proteins. Cocaine blocks these proteins, which results in high dopamine levels in the synaptic gap, and this can cause damage to brain cells.
